critic_raw:
Issue 1
Claim: The mortality comparison between November-December (model active) and July-September (historical comparison) shows that the model is improving care.
Why wrong: November-December and July-September are structurally different periods for emergency department case mix. Winter months see higher volume of respiratory illness, influenza, pneumonia, and cold-weather cardiovascular events. These conditions differ in acuity, sepsis risk, and mortality rates from the summer months. Comparing mortality across seasons without adjusting for case-mix differences is comparing unlike patient populations. A model that went live in November could show lower mortality simply because the winter-surge patient population, once treated under a strengthened antibiotic protocol, has better outcomes — not because of the model.
Evidence: Compare November-December case-mix characteristics (age, comorbidities, chief complaint distribution, illness severity scores) between the historical comparison year and the model-active year. Alternatively, compare the same November-December window from the prior year without the model.

Issue 2
Claim: The mortality improvement can be attributed to the model's alert functionality.
Why wrong: Two concurrent operational changes occurred at the same time as the model went live: (1) the hospital opened a winter surge unit, and (2) updated empiric antibiotic guidance for suspected sepsis was issued. The antibiotic protocol update alone could explain a large fraction of the mortality improvement — antibiotic timing and choice are among the strongest determinants of sepsis outcome. Without a way to isolate the model's contribution from these concurrent changes, attributing the mortality improvement to the alert is unjustified.
Evidence: Compare sepsis outcomes in emergency departments that received the model alert versus those that did not (if any existed), holding the antibiotic protocol constant. Or compare the model-active EDs versus a concurrent control ED that received the new antibiotic protocol without the model alert.

Issue 3
Claim: The analysis population (adults with early lactate testing) is a valid sample for measuring model impact.
Why wrong: The inclusion criterion depends on clinician behavior — ordering a lactate test within the first few hours of arrival. This behavior itself may have changed after the model went live or after the antibiotic protocol was updated. If the model or the protocol caused more clinicians to order lactate tests earlier, the included patient population at baseline (July-September) and during the model period (November-December) are not the same patient types — the model period may systematically include lower-acuity patients who were newly captured by the lactate ordering criterion.
Evidence: Compare the lactate ordering rate and the distribution of lactate values between the July-September and November-December cohorts. If the ordering rate or value distribution changed, the inclusion criterion selected different patient subsets.
defender_raw: The model provides real-time prioritization alerts to nurses, allowing faster review of high-risk patients. The alert itself did not change treatment but enabled faster response time. The internal validation showed the model correctly identifies high-risk patients. The November-December period shows improved outcomes and the hospital leadership attributes this to the model's implementation. The antibiotic protocol change was a background operational change; the key mechanism being tested is alert-based response speed.
adjudication_raw: The defender's response has three weaknesses. On Issue 1: the defender does not address the seasonal case-mix difference — the comparison crosses seasons without a concurrent control. On Issue 2: the defender acknowledges the antibiotic protocol change but dismisses it as 'background' without any evidence that it is not a primary driver. Antibiotic timing is among the strongest determinants of sepsis outcome; dismissing it as background is scientifically unjustified. On Issue 3: the defender does not address how lactate ordering rate changes could select different patient subsets. The defender's strongest point is internal model validation for prediction accuracy — but prediction accuracy does not establish downstream outcome impact. Critique wins.
all_issues_raised: Issue 1: seasonal case-mix difference between November-December and July-September comparison periods
Issue 2: concurrent antibiotic protocol update and winter surge unit explain mortality improvement independently of model
Issue 3: lactate ordering criterion may select different patient subsets across comparison periods

empirical_test.condition: Stepped-wedge or concurrent control group analysis: compare model-active EDs to comparable EDs without the model alert but with the same antibiotic protocol; compare the same November-December window from prior year as historical control
empirical_test.supports_critique_if: Concurrent control EDs with the new antibiotic protocol but without the model show similar mortality improvement; lactate ordering rates differ between comparison periods
empirical_test.supports_defense_if: Model-active EDs show lower mortality than concurrent control EDs with the same antibiotic protocol in the same season
empirical_test.ambiguous_if: Outcomes improve but effect cannot be cleanly separated from antibiotic protocol change; lactate ordering rate changed but direction of patient selection bias is unclear
